problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem D (operations research / data-asset theme): ICM, an intercontinental cargo seaport company, asks the team to (1) define indicators measuring the maturity of its Data & Analytics (D&A) system across people/technology/process, (2) build a model to score that maturity and prescribe how to improve it to "realize the full potential of data," (3) design a protocol to measure system effectiveness via the interrelationships among people/technology/process, and (4) test transferability to seaports of other sizes and industries, plus write a confidence-building letter to ICM users. There is no provided dataset — the team must construct its own indicator system and synthesize data.
models_used: DAMM — a bespoke Data Analysis system Maturity Model adapted from the Capability Maturity Model (CMM/Nolan), structured as a 3-dimension (People/Technology/Process) hierarchy of 17 indicators with 5 maturity levels
Grounded theory used as the qualitative method to derive/screen indicators from the maturity-model literature (Mettler, Tao, Lin, Xie, De Carolis)
AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) for subjective weighting, including pairwise judgment matrices and CR<0.1 consistency check
EWM (Entropy Weight Method) for objective weighting from information entropy
AHP-EWM combination weighting via Q_i = m*U_i + n*H_i, with m,n solved from the constraint that d(U,H)=d(m,n) and m+n=1
TOPSIS-style membership/normalization function to rescale the raw maturity score into [0,1] (final score 0.8173, level 4 of 5)
Linear programming (framed as goal programming) for Task 2: maximize optimized maturity score subject to a cost budget Δ, per-indicator cap (score<=1), and non-negative improvement constraints
System Dynamics (SD) simulation to generate synthetic indicator profiles for the 5 maturity levels and cross-validate against DAMM (MAPE 7.6%)
Apriori association-rule mining (min support 20%, min confidence 60%) for Task 3 to mine People/Technology/Process level -> maturity-level rules
Equal-width discretization to bin continuous SD outputs into discrete levels for Apriori
MAPE as the validation/error metric across Tasks 2 and 4
key_techniques: Combining subjective (AHP) and objective (EWM) weights to neutralize the bias of either method alone — a hallmark 'combined weighting' move for evaluation problems with no ground-truth weights
Solving the AHP/EWM blend coefficients m,n analytically by equating the Euclidean distance between weight vectors d(U,H) to |m-n| rather than picking m=n=0.5 arbitrarily — adds a defensible rationale
Reframing the open-ended 'realize full potential' Task 2 into a concrete constrained optimization: maximize maturity score s.t. cost budget, giving a quantitative improvement priority
Using System Dynamics to manufacture a synthetic dataset (5 maturity profiles) so that a data-hungry method (Apriori) becomes applicable despite the problem providing no real data
Closed-loop cross-validation: SD simulation value vs DAMM evaluation value compared with MAPE to claim the model is 'valid/stable' (7.6%, <10%)
Sensitivity analysis re-cast as the transferability test for Task 4: sweep relative seaport size 0.2–2.0, re-simulate, show error stays <5%
Radar-chart gap analysis between 'good' (level 4) and 'excellent' (level 5) profiles to pinpoint teamwork + talent/technical management as the highest-leverage investment
Heavy visualization layer — hierarchy diagrams, weight bar charts, radar charts, donut, line charts, Apriori flowchart — to make an abstract maturity model legible to judges
why_it_won: Problem D is data-light and concept-heavy: there is no dataset, so judges reward teams that build a coherent, defensible measurement framework rather than crunch numbers. This paper wins because it imposes a clean, literature-grounded structure (CMM-derived DAMM on the exact People/Technology/Process trichotomy the prompt names) and then chains a sensible method to each task: AHP-EWM for weighting, LP+SD for improvement, Apriori for the effectiveness protocol, sensitivity sweep for transfer. The combined-weighting and the m/n distance-matching derivation signal methodological care beyond plug-and-play AHP. Crucially it manufactures its own data (SD) so that every task produces concrete numbers (0.8173 score, MAPE 7.6%, errors <5%) and converts the vague 'realize full potential' ask into a constrained optimization with an actionable recommendation (invest in teamwork/talent and technical management). The work directly answers all four tasks plus the letter, is exhaustively visualized, and explicitly maps each modeling choice back to ICM's stated concerns — exactly the completeness-plus-narrative judges look for in an open-ended ICM problem.
section_highlights: Summary is task-by-task (Task1–Task4) and front-loads the headline number (0.8173, level 4) and the key recommendation — easy for a judge to grade in 30 seconds. Section 1.3 Literature Review traces a real lineage (Nolan 1979 -> CMM 1986 -> domain variants) that justifies the DAMM design rather than inventing it. Each indicator dimension (Figs 2–4: People/Technology/Process) gets its own relationship diagram tying indicators back to ICM's recruiting/tech/process questions. The weight bar chart (Fig 6) and radar 'good vs excellent' chart (Fig 10) carry the analytical punchlines visually. Strengths/Weaknesses section is honest (admits no real company data to validate against, only simulation-vs-model comparison). The closing letter to ICM seaport users restates the score and the teamwork/talent gap in plain, confidence-building language as required.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The entire pipeline is circular/self-validating: SD simulation is tuned to reproduce DAMM, then MAPE between SD and DAMM is presented as 'validation' — it validates internal consistency, not correctness against reality. The paper itself admits there is no actual company data to check against
Indicator scores, AHP pairwise judgments, and the cost coefficients b_i and budget Δ are essentially assumed/fabricated; results like 0.8173 inherit that arbitrariness and are not reproducible

Notation is sloppy and at points broken: formula (1) is stated as S_i = α + β_i*0.1 but the text earlier calls it α_1; several formulas contain literal '????' placeholders and a garbled entropy formula (E_j with f_ij vs w_ij mismatch); Apriori confidence values are inconsistent (table shows 57.14% and 85.17% but text says 85.71%)
The Apriori dataset is only 12 synthetic transactions, far too small for meaningful support/confidence; rules barely clear the 20% support / 60% confidence thresholds and several are near-trivial

Task 4 'model promotion' to other industries is asserted (people/tech/process are universal) rather than demonstrated; the sensitivity sweep only varies a 'relative seaport size' scalar, not genuine cross-industry structure
Linear programming in Task 2 is described in form only — no actual numbers are solved because b_i and Δ are 'assumed known'; the LP never produces a concrete optimized score
Some figures are mislabeled or inconsistent (e.g., Fig 5 donut 'values' are just an ascending 10–60 sequence with no clear meaning; process-level bin labels reuse B_1/B_2 where C_1.. is intended)
English is non-native throughout with grammatical errors that could distract a judge, though it remained clear enough to win
reusable_patterns: Maturity / evaluation problems with no data: anchor on an established framework (CMM, Nolan) and adapt it to the prompt's own named dimensions — instant credibility and structure

Combined weighting (AHP for subjective + EWM for objective, blended by Q=mU+nH) is a portable, judge-pleasing recipe for any multi-indicator scoring model; derive m,n via a distance-matching rule instead of defaulting to 0.5/0.5
Always run a consistency check (CR<0.1) on AHP matrices and show it — cheap rigor

Normalize the composite score with a TOPSIS-style min-max membership function and map to a labeled level scale (Fragile/Poor/Ordinary/Good/Excellent) so the output is interpretable, not just a raw number

When a method needs data the problem doesn't give (e.g., Apriori), use System Dynamics or Monte-Carlo to synthesize plausible scenarios per level, then mine them — and report MAPE between simulation and model as a sanity check

Turn an open-ended 'maximize potential / improve' task into an explicit constrained optimization (objective = score, constraints = budget + caps + monotonic improvement) to make recommendations quantitative
Use a radar-chart gap analysis between the current level and the target level to identify and justify the highest-leverage improvement levers
Recast 'does the model generalize?' as a sensitivity sweep over a scale parameter, plotting error vs parameter and showing it stays under a threshold (here <5%)
Give every dimension its own relationship diagram that explicitly answers the client's stated questions — strong narrative scaffolding that ties math back to the scenario
Front-load the headline result and the single actionable recommendation in the summary and again in the required letter, in plain language
